MORRISON & FOERSTER
SAN FRANCISCO
LOS ANGELES
ORANGE COUNTY
WALNUT CREEK
PALO ALTO

ATTORNEYS AT LAW

2000 PENNSYLVANIA AVENUE, N.Y.
WASHINGTON, D.C. 20006-1812
TELEPHONE (202) 887-1500
TELEFACSIMILE (202) 887-0763
TELEX 90-4030 MRSN FOERS WSH

NEW YORK
DENVER
LONDON
HONG KONG
TOKYO

DIRECT DIAL NUMBER

June 26, 1990

(202) 887-8739
after July 6--(714) 251-7500


Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political Practices
    Commission
428 J Street
Suite 800
P.O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804-0807

Re: Request for Informal Assistance

Dear Mr. Ahuja:

     As we have discussed on the telephone, this is a
request for informal written assistance from the Fair Political
Practices Commission under Regulation 18329(c).1/ We have been
authorized to request this assistance by a client whose
obligations under the Political Reform Act, Government Code
§ 81000 et seq. (the "Act"), are in question.

QUESTION

     Does a person who provides property tax consulting
services under contract to a land developer or other parties
(but not a government agency) in connection with the
establishment of a local bond financing district come within
the definition of a "consultant" under Regulation 18700(a)(2)?

PROPOSED CONCLUSION

     A person who has not entered into a contract with a
state or local government agency to provide information,
advice, recommendation or counsel to the government agency does

1/ All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6
of the California Code of Regulations.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
June 26, 1990
Page Two

not come within the definition of a "consultant" under
Regulation 18700(a)(2), even if the person provides information
or advice to a land developer or other parties (other than a
government agency) in connection with the establishment of a
local bond financing district.

ANALYSIS

Chapter 7 of the Act imposes certain conflict of
interest restrictions on "public officials" of state and local
governments. Cal. Gov't. Code § 87100 et seq.
Regulation 18700 defines a public official to include, among
others, a "consultant of a state or local government agency."
The regulation defines a consultant as:

any natural person who provides, under
contract, to a state or local government agency, information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to a state or
local government agency, . . . .

Regulation § 18700(a)(2) (emphasis added).2/

The definition of a consultant thus provides that a
consultant be a natural person who provides certain services
to a state or local government agency under contract with
the agency.

The Mello-Roos Community Facilities Act of 1982,
Government Code § 53311 et seq., provides for the
establishment of special bond financing districts known as
community facilities districts ("CFD"). Establishment of a
CFD enables a local government agency to raise funds through
the sale of bonds, the proceeds of which are used to finance
the construction of community facilities (for example, fire
stations, libraries, schools and parks) within the area
covered by the CFD. Id. §§ 53313, 53313.5. The bonds are
repaid by special taxes ("CFD taxes") levied on the real
property located within the CFD. Id. § 53358.

Under the provisions of the Government Code, a CFD
is established by the action of the relevant local
government agency. Id. § 53318 et seq. However, the
process of establishing a CFD typically is initiated by a
land developer or other private party who makes a proposal

2/ The regulation contains certain exclusions from the
definition of a consultant not relevant to this discussion.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
June 26, 1990
Page Three

to the relevant government agency to establish a CFD to
finance the construction of facilities in an area the
developer owns and wishes to develop. The developer's
proposal to the government agency typically includes a
description of the facilities to be constructed and an
estimate of the total costs of the construction. The
developer's presentation also may include a proposed "tax
spread," that is, a methodology for apportioning the CFD
taxes over the parcels of real property in the CFD.3/ If
the government agency wishes to proceed with the CFD, the
government agency and its counsel work with the developer,
bond counsel, a bond underwriter and others to complete the
plans for the CFD. Once completed and approved, the CFD
proposal must be approved in a special election before the
CFD can finally be established. Id. 53325.3.

Our client is a special tax consultant who designs
the tax spread methodology and performs the calculations
required in connection with a proposed CFD. (This is
accomplished by use of special computer software which our
client has developed.) This letter addresses cases in which
our client provides its services under contract with the
land developer or other private party (but not the
government agency) in connection with a proposed CFD. In
these cases, our client has no contract, written or oral,
with the government agency.

As discussed above, the definition of a consultant
under Regulation 18700(a)(2) provides that a consultant is a
person that provides certain services under contract with a
government agency. Because our client, under the facts set
forth above, has no contract with the government agency in
connection with his CFD work, he is not a consultant under
Regulation § 18700(a)(2).

*       *       *




3/ CFD taxes typically are apportioned based on the size of
the parcel, the size of the structure to be built on the
parcel, the density of the proposed development, the
increased traffic burden the development will cause or other
factors.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
June 26, 1990
Page Four


When you have received this letter, kindly stamp or
mark the enclosed copy as "received" and return the copy to
me in the enclosed stamped and self-addressed envelope for
our files.

Thank you for your consideration of this matter.
Should you have any questions, please do not hesitate to
telephone me.

Yours very truly,

Robert K. Olsen


P38464  15652(2)

MORRISON & FOERSTER
ATTORNEYS AT LAW

LOS ANGELES
SAN FRANCISCO
WALNUT CREEK
PALO ALTO
DENVER

19900 MacARTHUR BOULEVARD
IRVINE, CALIFORNIA 92715
TELEPHONE (714) 251-7500
TELEFACSIMILE (714) 251-0900

NEW YORK
WASHINGTON, D.C.
LONDON
HONG KONG
TOKYO

September 26, 1990


VIA FEDERAL EXPRESS


Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
Counsel, Legal Division
California Fair Political
Practices Commission
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, California 95804-0807

Re:  Supplement to Request For
     Informal Assistance

Dear Mr. Ahuja:

     Pursuant to our telephone conversation on August
29, this letter is to supplement our request for informal
written assistance dated June 26, 1990.

     As discussed more fully in our June 26 letter, it
is our position that a person who has not entered into a
contract with a state or local government agency to provide
information, advice, recommendation or counsel to the
government agency does not come within the definition of a
"consultant" under Regulation 18700(a)(2).1/ In our
telephone conversation, you asked us to provide more
information about the relationship our client has with the
public agency in connection with a proposed community
facilities district ("CFD"). You explained that the Fair
Political Practices Commission ("FPPC") staff had decided to
conduct a facts and circumstances review before rendering
advice on consultant conflicts of interest questions. This
was at least in part because of a recent situation in which
a city contracted with a land developer for the developer to
provide two staff members to the city to work on a land use

_____________________
1/ All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6
of the California Code of Regulations.

MORRISON & FOERSTER

Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
September 26, 1990
Page Two

application submitted by the developer. You explained that
although the two staff members were not under contract to
the city, the FPPC staff had taken the position that, on the
facts and circumstances of the case, the staff persons were
"consultants" under Regulation 18700 (who thus could not
continue to receive income from the developer while working
for the city on the developer's application).

In the case you described, the two staff members,
although not under an express contract to the city, clearly
were providing services to the city and served in the role
of city staff. Conflict of interest concerns may well be
raised where city staff members (or their functional
equivalent) are on the payroll of a private party whose land
use application is being handled by those staff members.
Our client's situation, however, is completely
distinguishable.

As discussed in our June 26 letter, our client is a
special tax consultant who designs the tax spread
methodology and performs the calculations for CFDs. Our
letter addressed the cases in which our client provides
services under contract with the relevant developer or other
private party, such as bond counsel (but not the government
agency) ("Contract Party"), in connection with a proposed
CFD. Our client has no contract, written or oral, express
or implied, with the government agency. Moreover, our
client does not act as the functional equivalent of the
public agency's staff. Our client works exclusively for the
Contract Party and submits its work product only to the
Contract Party. Once our client has submitted its work
product to the Contract Party, the work product becomes the
property of the Contract Party, and our client has no
further control over its disposition. The Contract Party
generally turns the work product over to the public agency
as part of the CFD proposal. The Contract Party may ask our
client to attend meetings with the Contract Party and the
public agency to answer the public agency's questions in
regard to the CFD proposal, but our client does not deal
with the public agency other than as a contractor to the
Contract Party.

In sum, our client has no written or oral
contractual arrangement with the public agency, functions
independently of the public agency and does not serve as the
functional equivalent of public agency staff. The public
agency is aware at all times that our client is working for
a private party and not the public agency. On these facts

MORRISON & FOERSTLER


Jeevan S. Ahuja, Esq.
September 26, 1990
Page Three


and circumstances and for the additional reasons discussed
in our June 26 letter, we submit that our client is not a
consultant under Regulation 18700.

     As always, please call me should you have any
further questions or comments.  Thank you for your
consideration.

                                        Very truly yours,


                                        [signature]
                                        Robert K. Olsen

RKO/fh

Iocal" bond financing district.
